$$a(i) = \frac{1}{\vert C_I \vert - 1} \sum_{j \in C_I, \, j \neq i} d(i, j)$$

**Inter-cluster separation** $b(i)$ — minimum average distance to points in any *other* cluster:

$$b(i) = \min_{J \neq I} \frac{1}{\vert C_J \vert} \sum_{j \in C_J} d(i, j)$$

**Silhouette:**

$$s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$$

**Interpretation:**

- $s(i) \approx +1$: $i$ is well-clustered (far from neighboring clusters).
- $s(i) \approx 0$: $i$ lies on the decision boundary.
- $s(i) \approx -1$: $i$ is likely mis-clustered.

## 3.4 Empirical Evaluation Pipeline (Analytical Matrix)

For the KTU lab record, the following computations should be tabulated for $K = 10$ on digits:

| **Metric** | **Formula in Code** | **Expected Range on Digits** |
|---|---|---|
| Inertia | `kmeans.inertia_` | $\approx 69{,}000$ – $72{,}000$ |
| Silhouette (mean) | `silhouette_score(X, labels)` | $\approx 0.18$ – $0.20$ |
| Homogeneity | `homogeneity_score(y, labels)` | $\approx 0.70$ – $0.75$ |
| Completeness | `completeness_score(y, labels)` | $\approx 0.72$ – $0.77$ |
| Adjusted Rand Index | `adjusted_rand_score(y, labels)` | $\approx 0.66$ – $0.72$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 K-Means Algorithm — Lloyd's Iteration Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start K-Means on Digits]) --> loadB[Load sklearn digits dataset 1797 samples 64 features]
    loadB --> scaleC[StandardScaler normalize pixel intensities]
    scaleC --> initD{K-Means++ initialization of K centroids}
    initD --> assignE[Step 1 Assign each x to nearest centroid argmin distance squared]
    assignE --> recomputeF[Step 2 Recompute centroids as mean of cluster members]
    recomputeF --> checkG{Convergence centroid shift le tol OR max iter reached}
    checkG -->|No| assignE
    checkG -->|Yes| evalH[Compute Inertia Silhouette ARI NMI]
    evalH --> vizI[Plot elbow curve and confusion matrix]
    vizI --> endJ([Return cluster labels and metrics])
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Digits → K-Means → Evaluation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT STAGE"]
        raw1[Raw Digits 8x8 grayscale images]
        feat2[Feature Matrix 1797 x 64]
    end

    subgraph PREP["PREPROCESSING STAGE"]
        norm3[StandardScaler zero mean unit variance]
        ready4[Scaled Feature Matrix]
    end

    subgraph KMEANS["KMEANS ENGINE"]
        seed5[KMeans plus plus seeding 10 centroids]
        lloyd6[Lloyd iteration Assign plus Update]
        converge7[Convergence detector inertia change le 1e-4]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT STAGE"]
        labels8[Cluster labels 0 to 9]
        centroids9[Learned centroids 10 x 64]
    end

    subgraph EVAL["EVALUATION STAGE"]
        inert10[Inertia WCSS]
        silh11[Silhouette Score]
        ari12[Adjusted Rand Index]
        nmi13[Normalized Mutual Info]
        confm14[Confusion Matrix 10x10]
    end

    raw1 --> feat2 --> norm3 --> ready4 --> seed5 --> lloyd6 --> converge7
    converge7 -->|loop| lloyd6
    converge7 --> labels8
    converge7 --> centroids9
    labels8 --> inert10
    labels8 --> silh11
    labels8 --> ari12
    labels8 --> nmi13
    labels8 --> confm14
```

## 4.3 Cluster Centroids Visualization (Conceptual)

> [!NOTE]
> When the 10 learned centroids (each a $64$-dim vector) are reshaped to $8 \times 8$ and rendered as grayscale images, they **visually resemble the prototypical digit** of their assigned cluster. This is a powerful qualitative validation of the experiment.

## 4.4 Reference Python Implementation Skeleton (Lab-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **lab-course (PCCSL508)** deliverable, so a minimal reference implementation is provided for the KTU record. It is **not** a substitute for the student's own hand-written program.

```python
import numpy as np
from sklearn.datasets import load_digits
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.metrics import (silhouette_score, adjusted_rand_score,
                             normalized_mutual_info_score, confusion_matrix)

# 1. Load dataset
X, y = load_digits(return_X_y=True)            # (1797, 64), labels 0-9

# 2. Preprocess
X_scaled = StandardScaler().fit_transform(X)

# 3. Fit K-Means with k-means++ initialization, K = 10
km = KMeans(n_clusters=10, init='k-means++',
            n_init=10, max_iter=300, tol=1e-4, random_state=42)
labels = km.fit_predict(X_scaled)

# 4. Evaluate
print("Inertia (WCSS)        :", km.inertia_)
print("Silhouette Score      :", silhouette_score(X_scaled, labels))
print("Adjusted Rand Index   :", adjusted_rand_score(y, labels))
print("NMI Score             :", normalized_mutual_info_score(y, labels))
print("Confusion Matrix:\n", confusion_matrix(y, labels))
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)**

### **Q1. Define K-Means clustering. List its two main iterative steps.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

K-Means clustering is an **unsupervised learning algorithm** that partitions $N$ data points into $K$ non-overlapping clusters by minimizing the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS / inertia)**.

The two iterative steps are:

1. **Cluster Assignment Step:** Each data point is assigned to the cluster whose centroid is closest in Euclidean distance:
$$c^{(j)} = \arg\min_i \;\Vert \mathbf{x}^{(j)} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i \Vert_2^2$$

2. **Centroid Update Step:** The centroid of each cluster is recomputed as the mean of all points currently assigned to it:
$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_i = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_i} \mathbf{x}$$

These steps are repeated until convergence.

---

### **Q2. What is the role of `k-means++` initialization? Why is it preferred over random initialization?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

`k-means++` is a smart seeding strategy that selects initial centroids to be **well-separated** in feature space. The first centroid is chosen uniformly at random; each subsequent centroid is selected with probability proportional to the **squared distance** from the nearest already-chosen centroid.

**Why preferred:**
- Random initialization can lead to **poor local optima**, **empty clusters**, or **slow convergence**.
- `k-means++` provides a theoretical guarantee that the expected WCSS is within $O(\log K)$ of the optimal solution.
- It **reduces sensitivity** to the `random_state` and produces **more stable, reproducible clusters**, which is essential when clustering the Digits dataset into 10 classes.

---

## **PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)**

> **INSTRUCTION:** Answer **ONE** of the following two full questions. Each carries **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a)** Explain the K-Means clustering algorithm in detail. Derive the mathematical expression for the optimal centroid position of a cluster. State the time complexity of the algorithm. `[7 marks]` `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**K-Means Algorithm (4 marks):**
K-Means is an unsupervised, partitional clustering algorithm. Given $N$ data points $\{\mathbf{x}^{(1)}, \dots, \mathbf{x}^{(N)}\}$ and a user-defined number of clusters $K$, it minimizes the objective:

$$J = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_i} \Vert \mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i \Vert_2^2$$

where $\boldsymbol{\mu}_i$ is the centroid of cluster $C_i$.

**Steps:**
1. Initialize $K$ centroids using `k-means++`.
2. **Assignment:** Assign each point to the nearest centroid using Euclidean distance.
3. **Update:** Recompute each centroid as the mean of its cluster members.
4. **Convergence check:** Stop when centroid shift $\le$ `tol` or `max_iter` is reached; else go to step 2.
5. The algorithm is guaranteed to converge (monotonically non-increasing $J$, bounded below by 0), but only to a **local** optimum.

**Derivation of Optimal Centroid (2 marks):** *(See SECTION 3.1)*

Setting $\partial J_i / \partial \mu_{i,m} = 0$ yields $\mu_{i,m} = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_i} x_m$, so vectorially:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_i = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_i} \mathbf{x}$$

**Time Complexity (1 mark):** Per-iteration complexity is $\mathcal{O}(K \cdot N \cdot d)$ and total complexity is $\mathcal{O}(I \cdot K \cdot N \cdot d)$ where $I$ = iterations, $N$ = samples, $d$ = dimensions, $K$ = clusters.

---

#### **(b)** For the **sklearn Digits dataset** ($N=1797$, $d=64$, 10 classes), describe the complete experimental procedure to apply K-Means and evaluate the resulting clusters. Justify the choice of $K=10$ and list all evaluation metrics with their expected trends. `[7 marks]` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Experimental Procedure (4 marks):**

1. **Load the dataset** using `from sklearn.datasets import load_digits` → returns feature matrix $\mathbf{X}$ of shape $(1797, 64)$ and target vector $\mathbf{y}$ of shape $(1797,)$ with values in $\{0, 1, \dots, 9\}$.
2. **Preprocess** with `StandardScaler` to zero-mean, unit-variance pixels; this prevents high-intensity pixels from dominating distance.
3. **Apply K-Means** with `n_clusters=10`, `init='k-means++'`, `n_init=10`, `random_state=42`.
4. **Inspect the learned centroids** by reshaping each to $8 \times 8$ and visualizing as grayscale images — they should resemble digit prototypes.
5. **Compute evaluation metrics** (3 marks):
   - **Inertia (WCSS):** Lower indicates tighter clusters. On digits, expect $\approx 69{,}000$–$72{,}000$ after scaling.
   - **Silhouette Score:** Range $[-1, +1]$; expect $\approx 0.18$–$0.20$ (digits are visually similar → not perfectly separable).
   - **Adjusted Rand Index (ARI):** Compares learned labels to true labels (using `y` only for evaluation). Expect $\approx 0.66$–$0.72$.
   - **Normalized Mutual Information (NMI):** Expect $\approx 0.70$–$0.75$.
   - **Confusion Matrix:** $10 \times 10$ matrix showing which true digit maps to which cluster (use `scipy.optimize.linear_sum_assignment` or manual Hungarian method to find the best permutation).
6. **Elbow method validation:** Plot inertia vs $K \in \{2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14\}$; the "elbow" near $K=10$ supports our choice.

**Justification of $K=10$ (1 mark):** The dataset has 10 ground-truth classes (digits 0–9). Although K-Means is unsupervised, we *a priori* know $K=10$ makes physical sense. We also use the elbow method to confirm.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse **inertia** (always decreases with more clusters, hence useless alone) with the **silhouette score** (which has a *meaningful maximum*). For a 7-mark question, you must **list each metric AND state what a "good" value looks like**. Writing "calculate accuracy" will lose **2 marks** — K-Means is unsupervised, so **accuracy does not directly apply**; use ARI/NMI instead.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative choice)*

#### **(a)** What are the limitations of K-Means clustering? For each limitation, suggest a practical workaround suitable for the Digits dataset experiment. `[7 marks]` `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

| **Limitation** | **Description** | **Workaround for Digits** |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Sensitivity to initialization | Random init can give poor local optima. | Use `init='k-means++'` and `n_init=10` (run 10 times, keep best inertia). |
| 2. Requires pre-specifying $K$ | User must know cluster count. | Apply the **elbow method** and **silhouette analysis** on inertia vs $K$. |
| 3. Assumes spherical, equal-variance clusters | Fails on elongated or irregular shapes. | Use **Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM)** or **Spectral Clustering** as alternatives. |
| 4. Sensitive to outliers | A single outlier pulls the centroid. | Apply **DBSCAN** (density-based) or pre-filter outliers using **Isolation Forest**. |
| 5. Sensitive to feature scaling | High-magnitude features dominate. | **Standardize** pixels with `StandardScaler` before fitting. |
| 6. Curse of dimensionality | Distance becomes meaningless in high $d$. | Digits has $d=64$ (manageable); for higher $d$, apply **PCA** first to reduce to $\approx 20$ components. |
| 7. Hard assignment only | A digit "5" misclassified gets 0 probability of being "5". | Use **Fuzzy C-Means** or **GMM** for soft (probabilistic) assignments. |

> **Awarding Marks:** 1 mark per correctly identified limitation + workaround, max 7.

---

#### **(b)** Consider the following scenario: After running K-Means with $K=10$ on the Digits dataset, the **silhouette score is only 0.18**, but the **Adjusted Rand Index is 0.71**. Explain why both values are simultaneously true, and propose **two concrete improvements** to raise the silhouette score without sacrificing the ARI. `[7 marks]` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Interpretation of the two values (3 marks):**

- The **silhouette score (0.18)** measures *geometric* cluster separation in the original 64-dimensional pixel space. Many digits (e.g., "3" vs "8", "4" vs "9", "1" vs "7") are **visually overlapping** in raw pixel space, so the geometric clusters overlap → low silhouette.
- The **Adjusted Rand Index (0.71)** measures *label-alignment* by comparing cluster assignments to the true digit labels. It can be high even if geometric separation is weak, as long as each cluster is **predominantly one true digit** with only minor boundary confusion.

**Both can be true because** silhouette measures *intra/inter-cluster distance ratios* in feature space, while ARI measures *label purity agreement* — they are not directly comparable. A high ARI with low silhouette simply means the clusters are *topologically impure* (touching/overlapping) but *semantically aligned*.

**Two Concrete Improvements (4 marks — 2 marks each):**

1. **Dimensionality Reduction with PCA (or t-SNE for visualization):** Apply `PCA(n_components=20)` to retain 95% variance. This reduces noise, suppresses irrelevant pixel variation, and makes Euclidean distances more meaningful. Expect silhouette to rise to **$\approx 0.22$–$0.25$** while ARI stays similar.

2. **Normalize + Use Cosine Distance** *(advanced)*: Some digits differ more in *shape proportions* than in absolute pixel intensity. Switch to `KMeans(..., metric='cosine')` (using `scikit-learn-extra`) to focus on shape, not illumination. This often yields silhouette $\approx 0.25$+ and ARI improvements.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q5b):** Many students incorrectly state that "silhouette and ARI contradict each other" and try to *fix* one by changing $K$. **Do not** change $K$ — the question explicitly says "without sacrificing ARI." The valid answers involve **preprocessing improvements** (PCA, scaling, distance metric). Suggesting "use more clusters" will be marked **wrong**.

---

# **TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Lab Exam:**

- **K-Means** is **unsupervised** — labels $\mathbf{y}$ are used *only for evaluation*, never for training.
- **Objective Function:** $\;J = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_i} \Vert \mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i \Vert_2^2\;$ — always use $\Vert \cdot \Vert_2^2$ (no $\sqrt$).
- **Centroid formula:** $\;\boldsymbol{\mu}_i = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_i} \mathbf{x}\;$ — derived by setting $\partial J / \partial \boldsymbol{\mu}_i = 0$.
- **Assignment formula:** $\;c^{(j)} = \arg\min_i \Vert \mathbf{x}^{(j)} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i \Vert_2^2\;$.
- **k-means++** is the default initialization in sklearn — **always use it**; combined with `n_init=10` it almost guarantees a good local optimum.
- **Convergence is guaranteed** to a local (not global) optimum; WCSS monotonically non-increasing.
- **Digits dataset:** $\;N=1797,\;d=64,\;K=10\;$ (0–9), pixel range 0–16.
- **Preprocessing is mandatory:** `StandardScaler` before K-Means.
- **Evaluation metrics:** Inertia (always decreases with $K$, use elbow method), **Silhouette** (range $[-1,+1]$, higher is better), **ARI** and **NMI** (require true labels, range $[0,1]$, higher is better).
- **Do NOT** report "accuracy" for K-Means — it is unsupervised.
- **Time complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(I \cdot K \cdot N \cdot d)$ per run.
- **Empty cluster handling:** In sklearn, the empty cluster's centroid is re-initialized to the point farthest from any centroid.
- **Confusion matrix for K-Means:** Cluster IDs are arbitrary; use the **Hungarian algorithm** (`scipy.optimize.linear_sum_assignment`) to find the optimal 1-to-1 mapping between clusters and true digits before computing accuracy.
- **Common errors to avoid in the lab record:** (i) Forgetting to scale features, (ii) using `random_state=None`, (iii) reporting inertia alone without silhouette/ARI, (iv) computing accuracy directly without permuting cluster labels.
- **Code skeleton to memorize:** `load_digits()` → `StandardScaler()` → `KMeans(n_clusters=10, init='k-means++', n_init=10, random_state=42)` → `fit_predict(X)` → `silhouette_score`, `adjusted_rand_score`, `confusion_matrix`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bootstrapping and Cross-Validation on Iris Dataset
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCSL508 - MACHINE LEARNING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Bootstrapping and Cross-Validation on Iris Dataset

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions

> [!NOTE]
> **Bootstrapping** is a non-parametric statistical resampling technique introduced by **Bradley Efron (1979)** that estimates the sampling distribution of an estimator by sampling with replacement from the original dataset to create many simulated samples (called *bootstrap samples*). Each bootstrap sample has the same size *n* as the original dataset but contains duplicate observations and omits some.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Validation (CV)** is a systematic model assessment procedure in which the dataset is partitioned into complementary subsets: one subset is used for **training** the model and the other for **validation (testing)**. This cycle is repeated multiple times, and the results are averaged to produce a single, robust estimation of the model's generalization performance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Iris Dataset** is a multivariate, balanced benchmark dataset introduced by British statistician **Ronald A. Fisher (1936)**. It contains **150 samples** from **3 species** of Iris flowers (*Iris setosa*, *Iris versicolor*, *Iris virginica*) with **4 features**: sepal length, sepal width, petal length, and petal width (all in **cm**).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bootstrapping Analogy — "The Soup Taster":**
> Imagine you have a single small bowl of soup and want to estimate the *average saltiness of the entire kitchen's soup*. Since you cannot taste every pot, you take **one spoonful, then put it back, stir, and take another spoonful**. You do this **1,000 times**. Because you put the spoon back, each tasting is independent. By averaging your 1,000 estimates, you get a reliable picture of the true average saltiness — even though you started with just one bowl. That is **bootstrapping**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-Validation Analogy — "The Mock Tests Before Finals":**
> Suppose you have 10 question papers from last year and want to know how well-prepared you are. Instead of studying 9 and testing yourself on only 1, you study 9 in a *different combination* every time and test on the remaining 1. After **10 rounds**, every paper has been a test exactly once, and you average your scores. That is **10-Fold Cross-Validation** — you squeeze maximum evaluation information out of a limited dataset.

### 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Resampling** | Repeatedly drawing samples from observed data to assess variability. |
| **Sample with Replacement** | After drawing an observation, it is returned to the pool, so it can be drawn again. |
| **Out-of-Bag (OOB) Samples** | The ~36.8% of observations *not* picked in a given bootstrap sample. |
| **Fold** | One partition of the dataset in cross-validation. |
| **Holdout Set** | The single chunk reserved for testing in each fold. |
| **Bias–Variance Trade-off** | Core tension: Bootstrap reduces variance; CV gives an *unbiased* estimate of test error. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Bootstrapping — Step by Step

- **Step 1 — Original Sample:** Start with an original dataset $D = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$ of size $n$.
- **Step 2 — Generate Bootstrap Sample $D^{*(b)}$:** Draw $n$ observations **with replacement** uniformly at random from $D$. Denote this the $b$-th bootstrap sample.
- **Step 3 — Compute Statistic:** For each $D^{*(b)}$, compute the statistic of interest $\hat{\theta}^{*(b)}$ (e.g., mean, accuracy, coefficient).
- **Step 4 — Repeat:** Execute Steps 2–3 for $B$ iterations (typically $B = 1000$ or $10000$).
- **Step 5 — Aggregate:** Form the **bootstrap distribution** $\{\hat{\theta}^{*(1)}, \hat{\theta}^{*(2)}, \dots, \hat{\theta}^{*(B)}\}$ from which confidence intervals and standard errors are derived.

### 2.2 Operational Logic of k-Fold Cross-Validation

- **Step 1 — Shuffle & Partition:** Randomly shuffle the dataset and split it into $k$ mutually exclusive subsets (folds) of approximately equal size $\lfloor n/k \rfloor$.
- **Step 2 — Iterate:** For each fold $i \in \{1, 2, \dots, k\}$:
    - Treat fold $i$ as the **validation set**.
    - Treat the remaining $k-1$ folds as the **training set**.
    - Fit the model on training data; record the validation score $S_i$ (e.g., accuracy, MSE).
- **Step 3 — Average:** Compute the cross-validated estimate $\text{CV}_{(k)} = \dfrac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} S_i$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Probability an observation is *not* chosen in a bootstrap sample | $P(\text{not chosen}) = \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{n}\right)^{n}$ | OOB fraction calculation |
| Limit of OOB fraction as $n \to \infty$ | $\displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty}\left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^n = e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$ | Shows ~36.8% data is OOB |
| Bootstrap estimate of standard error | $\widehat{\text{SE}}_{B}(\hat{\theta}) = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{B-1} \sum_{b=1}^{B}\left(\hat{\theta}^{*(b)} - \bar{\theta}^{*}\right)^{2}}$ | Quantifies estimator variability |
| Bootstrap mean of statistic | $\bar{\theta}^{*} = \dfrac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{\theta}^{*(b)}$ | Point estimate of parameter |
| k-Fold CV score | $\text{CV}_{(k)} = \dfrac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} S_i$ | Model performance estimate |
| Leave-One-Out CV (LOOCV) | $\text{CV}_{(n)} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i$ | Special case: $k = n$ |
| Stratified k-Fold | Preserves class ratio $\dfrac{n_c}{n}$ in each fold | Used for imbalanced Iris variants |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real-world machine-learning pipelines, bootstrapping powers **Bagging** ensembles (e.g., Random Forests), while cross-validation is the **gold standard** for hyperparameter tuning (e.g., GridSearchCV, RandomizedSearchCV in scikit-learn). Together, they form the backbone of robust model evaluation in production AI systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Probability That an Observation is *Not* Selected in a Bootstrap Sample

Let us rigorously derive the famous **36.8% OOB fraction**.

**Step 1 — Probability of Selection per Draw:**
The probability that a specific observation $x_i$ is selected in a *single* draw from $n$ items (with replacement) is:

$$
P(\text{selected in one draw}) = \frac{1}{n}
$$

**Step 2 — Probability of *Non*-Selection per Draw:**
The complement is:

$$
P(\text{not selected in one draw}) = 1 - \frac{1}{n}
$$

**Step 3 — Independence Across $n$ Draws:**
Because we sample **with replacement**, each draw is independent. The bootstrap sample consists of $n$ such independent draws. The probability that $x_i$ is *never* selected across all $n$ draws is:

$$
P(x_i \text{ not in bootstrap sample}) = \left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^{n}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the Limit as $n \to \infty$:**
Using the standard exponential limit definition $e^x = \displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty}\left(1 + \frac{x}{n}\right)^{n}$, substitute $x = -1$:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty}\left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^{n} = e^{-1} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.36787
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
Therefore, **approximately 36.8% of the original data** never appears in any given bootstrap sample — these are the **Out-of-Bag (OOB)** observations, which can serve as a free validation set in algorithms like Random Forest.

### 3.2 Derivation: Bootstrap Standard Error

Given $B$ bootstrap statistics $\hat{\theta}^{*(1)}, \hat{\theta}^{*(2)}, \dots, \hat{\theta}^{*(B)}$,

**Step 1 — Bootstrap Mean:**

$$
\bar{\theta}^{*} = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{\theta}^{*(b)}
$$

**Step 2 — Squared Deviations:**
For each bootstrap replicate, compute:

$$
\left(\hat{\theta}^{*(b)} - \bar{\theta}^{*}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Sample Variance (Bessel's correction with $B-1$):**

$$
\widehat{\text{Var}}_{B}(\hat{\theta}) = \frac{1}{B-1} \sum_{b=1}^{B}\left(\hat{\theta}^{*(b)} - \bar{\theta}^{*}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Take Square Root for Standard Error:**

$$
\widehat{\text{SE}}_{B}(\hat{\theta}) = \sqrt{\widehat{\text{Var}}_{B}(\hat{\theta})} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{B-1} \sum_{b=1}^{B}\left(\hat{\theta}^{*(b)} - \bar{\theta}^{*}\right)^{2}}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Stratified 5-Fold Split on Iris (Sketch)

For Iris with $n = 150$ samples and $k = 5$ folds, each fold has $30$ samples. Because the dataset is perfectly balanced (50 per class), stratified sampling preserves **10 samples per class per fold**.

| Fold $i$ | Train Indices | Test Indices | Train Size | Test Size | Approx. Class Split in Test |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 30–150 | 1–30 | 120 | 30 | 10 setosa / 10 versicolor / 10 virginica |
| 2 | 1–30, 61–150 | 31–60 | 120 | 30 | 10 / 10 / 10 |
| 3 | 1–60, 91–150 | 61–90 | 120 | 30 | 10 / 10 / 10 |
| 4 | 1–90, 121–150 | 91–120 | 120 | 30 | 10 / 10 / 10 |
| 5 | 1–120 | 121–150 | 120 | 30 | 10 / 10 / 10 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Final CV Score:** $\text{CV}_{(5)} = \dfrac{1}{5}(S_1 + S_2 + S_3 + S_4 + S_5)$, where each $S_i$ is the model's accuracy on its respective held-out fold.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Block Diagram: Bootstrapping + Cross-Validation Pipeline on Iris

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Iris Dataset N equals 150] --> B[Step 1 Load CSV via Pandas]
    B --> C[Step 2 Separate Features X and Target y]
    C --> D{Choose Evaluation Path}

    D --> E[Bootstrapping Path]
    D --> F[Cross Validation Path]

    E --> E1[Loop B times typically 1000]
    E1 --> E2[Draw N samples with replacement]
    E2 --> E3[Fit Classifier e.g. DecisionTree]
    E3 --> E4[Predict on Out of Bag samples]
    E4 --> E5[Store accuracy score]
    E5 --> E6[Compute mean and std of accuracies]

    F --> F1[Choose k typically 5 or 10]
    F1 --> F2[Split data into k stratified folds]
    F2 --> F3[For each fold i]
    F3 --> F4[Train on k minus 1 folds]
    F4 --> F5[Test on fold i record score S i]
    F5 --> F6{All k folds done}
    F6 --> F7[Compute average CV accuracy]
    F6 --> F3

    E6 --> G[Final Report Mean Accuracy and Std Dev]
    F7 --> G
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Diagram: 5-Fold Cross-Validation Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Fold 1 Test] --> A2[Fold 2 Test]
    A2 --> A3[Fold 3 Test]
    A3 --> A4[Fold 4 Test]
    A4 --> A5[Fold 5 Test]
    A5 --> A6[Average Score]
    B1[Train Data 4 Folds] -.-> A1
    B2[Train Data 4 Folds] -.-> A2
    B3[Train Data 4 Folds] -.-> A3
    B4[Train Data 4 Folds] -.-> A4
    B5[Train Data 4 Folds] -.-> A5
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Diagram: Bootstrap Sample Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Dataset of size N] --> B[Draw random index i1 in 1 to N]
    B --> C[Record sample at i1]
    C --> D{Repeat N times with replacement}
    D --> E[Bootstrap Sample of size N]
    E --> F[Contains duplicates and omits ~36.8 percent]
    F --> G[Use for model fitting or statistic estimation]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**
> **Q1.** Define bootstrapping. Why is sampling done *with replacement*?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Bootstrapping is a resampling technique used to estimate the sampling distribution of a statistic by drawing multiple samples **with replacement** from the original dataset. Sampling *with replacement* ensures that each draw is independent and that the bootstrap sample has the same size $n$ as the original dataset, allowing us to simulate the variability of the statistic without assuming any underlying parametric distribution. **(3 marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
> **Q2.** What is the Out-of-Bag (OOB) fraction in bootstrapping? State its limiting value.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The OOB fraction refers to the proportion of original observations that are *not* selected in a particular bootstrap sample. Because sampling is with replacement, the probability that a given observation is never selected in $n$ draws is $\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{n}\right)^{n}$. As $n \to \infty$, this converges to $e^{-1} \approx \mathbf{0.3679}$ or **36.79%**. These OOB samples can be reused as a built-in validation set. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – University Model Paper Pattern]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**a)** Explain the procedure of k-Fold Cross-Validation. For the Iris dataset ($n=150$, 3 classes of 50 each), compute the expected size of the training and test sets for $k=5$ and $k=10$ folds. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- **Procedure (5 marks):** k-Fold Cross-Validation partitions the dataset into $k$ equally-sized folds. In iteration $i$ ($i = 1$ to $k$), fold $i$ acts as the test set while the remaining $k-1$ folds form the training set. The model is trained and evaluated; the score $S_i$ is recorded. After all $k$ iterations, the cross-validated estimate is $\text{CV}_{(k)} = \dfrac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} S_i$.
- **Computation for $k=5$ (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Test size} = \left\lfloor \frac{150}{5} \right\rfloor = 30, \quad \text{Train size} = 150 - 30 = 120
$$

- **Computation for $k=10$ (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Test size} = \left\lfloor \frac{150}{10} \right\rfloor = 15, \quad \text{Train size} = 150 - 15 = 135
$$

**b)** With a neat diagram, describe how bootstrapping is used to estimate the standard error of the mean petal length of Iris-versicolor samples. Mention the role of the OOB samples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- **Step 1:** From the 50 Iris-versicolor samples, draw $B$ bootstrap samples (each of size 50) with replacement.
- **Step 2:** For each bootstrap sample $b$, compute the mean petal length $\bar{x}^{*(b)}$.
- **Step 3:** Compute the bootstrap estimate of standard error using:

$$
\widehat{\text{SE}}_{B} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{B-1}\sum_{b=1}^{B}\left(\bar{x}^{*(b)} - \bar{x}^{*}\right)^{2}}
$$

- **Step 4:** The OOB samples (those not present in a given bootstrap sample) can be used to validate the trained classifier without needing a separate holdout set, providing an unbiased estimate of generalization error. **(7 marks)**

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**a)** Compare and contrast Bootstrapping and Cross-Validation with respect to their purpose, sampling strategy, computational cost, and primary use case in machine-learning pipelines. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Tabular Form – 7 marks):**

| Aspect | Bootstrapping | Cross-Validation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Purpose** | Estimate variability (SE, CI) of a statistic | Estimate generalization/test error of a model |
| **Sampling** | With replacement | Without replacement (mutually exclusive folds) |
| **Sample Size** | Each sample = $n$ | Each train fold $\approx (k-1)n/k$, test = $n/k$ |
| **Data Reuse** | Each observation may appear multiple times | Each observation appears in exactly one test fold |
| **Computational Cost** | High if $B$ and model are expensive (typically $B=1000$) | High if $k$ and model are expensive (typically $k=5$ or $10$) |
| **Primary Use** | Bagging ensembles, confidence intervals | Hyperparameter tuning, model selection |
| **Validation Mechanism** | OOB samples | Held-out folds |

**b)** Write the step-by-step algorithm (pseudocode or scikit-learn workflow) to perform **Stratified 10-Fold Cross-Validation** on the Iris dataset using a **Decision Tree classifier**, and report the expected output structure (per-fold accuracy, mean, std). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Import libraries: `from sklearn.datasets import load_iris`, `from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier`, `from sklearn.model_selection import StratifiedKFold, cross_val_score`.
2. Load Iris dataset and extract `X` (features, shape $150 \times 4$) and `y` (labels).
3. Initialize the classifier: `clf = DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=42)`.
4. Create the stratified splitter: `skf = StratifiedKFold(n_splits=10, shuffle=True, random_state=42)`.
5. Call: `scores = cross_val_score(clf, X, y, cv=skf, scoring='accuracy')`.
6. The output `scores` is an array of 10 values: `[S_1, S_2, \dots, S_{10}]`.
7. Final report:

$$
\text{Mean Accuracy} = \frac{1}{10}\sum_{i=1}^{10} S_i, \quad \text{Std Dev} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{9}\sum_{i=1}^{10}(S_i - \bar{S})^2}
$$

Typical Iris Decision Tree results: **Mean ≈ 0.95, Std ≈ 0.04**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing OOB with cross-validation test set** — OOB is *only* from bootstrap; CV test folds are explicitly held out.
> 2. **Forgetting stratification** — On balanced datasets like Iris it's automatic, but on imbalanced data random splitting causes bias.
> 3. **Reporting raw accuracy without mean & std** — KTU examiners explicitly look for **both central tendency and dispersion**.
> 4. **Misapplying LOOCV on Iris** — LOOCV ($k=n$) on 150 samples means 150 fits; computationally wasteful when $k=5$ or $k=10$ suffices.
> 5. **Setting `random_state=None`** — Results become non-reproducible; always fix the seed for KTU lab records.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Bootstrapping** = *resampling with replacement* to estimate **statistic variability** (SE, CI, bias).
- **Cross-Validation** = *partitioning without replacement* to estimate **model generalization error**.
- **OOB fraction** $\to e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$, i.e., **~36.8%** of original data is left out per bootstrap sample.
- **Iris dataset**: $n = 150$, **3 balanced classes** of 50 each, **4 features** in cm.
- **k-Fold formula**: $\text{CV}_{(k)} = \dfrac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} S_i$; for Iris with $k=5$, each fold has **30 samples**.
- **Stratified k-Fold** preserves class ratios $\dfrac{n_c}{n}$ — **essential** for imbalanced data, beneficial for Iris.
- **Bagging** (Bootstrap Aggregating) is built on bootstrapping; **GridSearchCV** is built on cross-validation.
- **Typical KTU expected output**: Mean accuracy ± Standard Deviation (e.g., $0.95 \pm 0.04$).
- **Always set `random_state`** for reproducibility in KTU lab records and university exams.
- **Common classifiers used with Iris in this experiment**: Decision Tree, KNN, Logistic Regression, SVM.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bagging and Boosting on Titanic Dataset
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCSL508 - MACHINE LEARNING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Bagging and Boosting on Titanic Dataset

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Ensemble Learning** is a meta-learning paradigm in which multiple base learners (weak/moderate classifiers) are strategically combined to produce a single composite predictor that generalizes better than any of its individual constituents. Within the KTU 2024 scheme framework for **PCCSL508 – Machine Learning Lab**, Experiment 2 mandates the empirical comparison of two principal ensemble paradigms — **Bagging (Bootstrap AGGregatING)** and **Boosting (Sequential Adaptive Reweighting)** — on the classic **Titanic Survival Dataset**, a binary classification problem aimed at predicting whether a passenger survived the 1912 shipwreck.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – Ensemble Methods):** Students must implement Bagging and at least two Boosting variants (typically **AdaBoost** and **Gradient Boosting**, with **XGBoost** as an industry-standard extension) on the Titanic dataset, perform hyperparameter tuning, and tabulate comparative performance metrics.

**Bagging (Breiman, 1996)** trains $B$ independent base classifiers on $B$ different bootstrap samples drawn *with replacement* from the original training set of size $N$, and aggregates their predictions via **majority voting** (classification) or **averaging** (regression). Its primary mechanism is **variance reduction** without materially increasing bias.

**Boosting (Freund & Schapire, 1997)** trains base classifiers **sequentially**, where each subsequent learner focuses disproportionately on the instances misclassified (or poorly predicted) by the previous ensemble, with instance weights updated iteratively. Its primary mechanism is **bias reduction** (and simultaneous variance reduction when regularized).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

### The Bagging Analogy: "The Jury of Independent Doctors"
> [!NOTE]
> Imagine 50 doctors are each given a *different random subset* of patient records (some records repeated, some omitted). Each doctor independently diagnoses. The final diagnosis is the **majority vote**. Because each doctor saw a slightly different mix of cases, their individual mistakes are *uncorrelated* and tend to cancel out. This is **Bagging** — many independent, parallel opinions averaged together.

### The Boosting Analogy: "The Iterative Tutor"
> [!NOTE]
> Imagine a single tutor giving a student a series of mock tests. After each test, the tutor **identifies the questions the student got wrong** and creates the *next* mock test heavily weighted with those weak topics. The student gradually corrects weaknesses. This is **Boosting** — sequential learners, each fixing the mistakes of the previous one.

## 1.3 The Titanic Dataset — Context

The **Titanic dataset** (Kaggle / seaborn built-in) contains demographic and ticket information for **891 passengers** in the training set. The target variable is `Survived` (binary: 1 = survived, 0 = perished). The key features are:

- `Pclass` (Ticket class: 1, 2, 3) — proxy for socio-economic status
- `Sex` (male, female)
- `Age` (numeric, has missing values)
- `SibSp` (number of siblings/spouses aboard)
- `Parch` (number of parents/children aboard)
- `Fare` (ticket price)
- `Embarked` (port of embarkation: C, Q, S)

Standard baseline accuracy is approximately **80%** for a well-tuned ensemble.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Benchmark Metric:** The Kaggle Titanic leaderboard's logistic regression baseline is ~**0.767**. A properly tuned Random Forest (bagging of decision trees) typically achieves **0.82–0.85**, while XGBoost (boosting) reaches **0.87–0.89**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics of Bagging

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. Given training set $D = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$.
2. For $b = 1, 2, \ldots, B$ (number of base learners):
   - Draw a **bootstrap sample** $D_b$ of size $N$ from $D$ *with replacement*.
   - Train a base classifier $h_b(x)$ on $D_b$.
3. **Aggregate** the predictions:
   - Classification: $\hat{y} = \text{mode}\{h_1(x), h_2(x), \ldots, h_B(x)\}$ (majority vote).
   - Regression: $\hat{y} = \frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B} h_b(x)$ (averaging).

### Why Bagging Works — Variance Reduction Intuition

If each base learner has variance $\sigma^2$ and pairwise correlation $\rho$, then the variance of the averaged ensemble of $B$ independent models is:

$$
\text{Var}_{\text{ensemble}} = \rho \cdot \sigma^2 + \frac{1-\rho}{B} \cdot \sigma^2
$$

As $B \to \infty$, the second term vanishes, leaving a residual variance governed by $\rho$. **The more uncorrelated the base learners, the greater the variance reduction.** This is why Bagging excels with **high-variance, low-bias** base learners (e.g., unpruned decision trees).

## 2.2 Operational Mechanics of Boosting

### 2.2.1 AdaBoost (Adaptive Boosting)

AdaBoost reweights training instances after each iteration. Misclassified points gain weight, correctly classified points lose weight.

**Initialization** (iteration $t = 0$):
$$
w_i^{(0)} = \frac{1}{N}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, N
$$

**Weighted Error** of classifier $h_t$:
$$
\epsilon_t = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i^{(t)} \cdot \mathbb{1}\{h_t(x_i) \neq y_i\}
$$

**Learner Weight (Confidence)**:
$$
\alpha_t = \frac{1}{2} \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - \epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t}\right)
$$

**Instance Weight Update**:
$$
w_i^{(t+1)} = w_i^{(t)} \cdot \exp\!\big(-\alpha_t \, y_i \, h_t(x_i)\big)
$$

followed by normalization $\sum_i w_i^{(t+1)} = 1$.

**Final Ensemble Prediction**:
$$
H(x) = \text{sign}\!\left(\sum_{t=1}^{T} \alpha_t \, h_t(x)\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When $\epsilon_t = 0.5$ (random guessing), $\alpha_t = 0$ (the learner is discarded). When $\epsilon_t \to 0$, $\alpha_t \to \infty$ (the learner is heavily trusted).

### 2.2.2 Gradient Boosting

Instead of reweighting instances, **Gradient Boosting (Friedman, 2001)** fits each new base learner to the **negative gradient** of a chosen loss function $L(y, F(x))$ with respect to the current ensemble prediction $F_{t-1}(x)$:

$$
r_{i}^{(t)} = -\left[\frac{\partial L(y_i, F(x_i))}{\partial F(x_i)}\right]_{F = F_{t-1}}
$$

For **squared error** loss, $r_i^{(t)} = y_i - F_{t-1}(x_i)$ — i.e., fit the **residuals**. The new ensemble is updated as:
$$
F_t(x) = F_{t-1}(x) + \nu \cdot h_t(x)
$$
where $\nu$ is the **learning rate** (shrinkage factor), typically $\nu \in [0.01, 0.3]$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Bootstrap sample size | $N$ | Same as original; drawn with replacement |
| Bagging variance | $\rho \sigma^2 + \dfrac{(1-\rho)\sigma^2}{B}$ | $\rho$ = pairwise base-learner correlation |
| AdaBoost init weight | $w_i^{(0)} = \dfrac{1}{N}$ | Uniform distribution |
| AdaBoost error | $\epsilon_t = \sum_i w_i^{(t)} \mathbb{1}\{h_t(x_i) \neq y_i\}$ | Weighted misclassification rate |
| AdaBoost $\alpha_t$ | $\alpha_t = \dfrac{1}{2} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1-\epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t}\right)$ | Higher $\alpha$ for lower $\epsilon$ |
| Instance weight update | $w_i^{(t+1)} \propto w_i^{(t)} \exp(-\alpha_t y_i h_t(x_i))$ | Up-weight misclassified points |
| Gradient Boosting residual | $r_i^{(t)} = y_i - F_{t-1}(x_i)$ | For squared error loss |
| Ensemble update | $F_t(x) = F_{t-1}(x) + \nu h_t(x)$ | $\nu$ = learning rate |
| OOB error estimate | $\approx \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}\{y_i \neq \hat{y}_i^{\text{oob}}\}$ | Free validation in Bagging |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fraud detection** in banking — boosting variants dominate due to imbalanced datasets.
- **Medical diagnosis** — bagging stabilizes noisy patient-record predictions.
- **Credit scoring** — XGBoost is the de-facto industry standard.
- **Kaggle competitions** — Gradient Boosting (XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost) wins the majority of structured-data contests.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Variance Reduction in Bagging

**Setup:** Suppose the $B$ base learners produce predictions $h_1(x), h_2(x), \ldots, h_B(x)$ with identical true function $f(x)$ but independent random noise with variance $\sigma^2$ and pairwise correlation coefficient $\rho$.

The ensemble prediction is the average:
$$
\bar{h}(x) = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} h_b(x)
$$

The variance of the ensemble is:
$$
\text{Var}[\bar{h}(x)] = \text{Var}\!\left[\frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} h_b(x)\right]
$$

Using the identity $\text{Var}[aX] = a^2 \text{Var}[X]$ and expanding:
$$
\text{Var}[\bar{h}(x)] = \frac{1}{B^2} \left[ \sum_{b=1}^{B} \text{Var}[h_b(x)] + \sum_{b \neq k} \text{Cov}[h_b(x), h_k(x)] \right]
$$

Because the base learners are identically distributed, $\text{Var}[h_b(x)] = \sigma^2$ for all $b$, and $\text{Cov}[h_b, h_k] = \rho \sigma^2$ for all $b \neq k$. Substituting:
$$
\text{Var}[\bar{h}(x)] = \frac{1}{B^2}\!\left[ B \sigma^2 + B(B-1) \rho \sigma^2 \right]
$$

Simplifying the algebraic step:
$$
\text{Var}[\bar{h}(x)] = \frac{\sigma^2}{B^2}\!\left[ B + B(B-1)\rho \right] = \frac{\sigma^2}{B}\!\left[ 1 + (B-1)\rho \right]
$$

Regrouping the result:
$$
\text{Var}[\bar{h}(x)] = \rho \sigma^2 + \frac{(1 - \rho)\sigma^2}{B}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limit cases:** If $\rho = 0$ (independent learners), $\text{Var}[\bar{h}] = \sigma^2/B \to 0$ as $B \to \infty$ — full variance annihilation. If $\rho = 1$ (perfectly correlated learners), $\text{Var}[\bar{h}] = \sigma^2$ — no benefit from ensembling.

## 3.2 Derivation: AdaBoost Weight Update

**Goal:** Show that the AdaBoost weight update is the multiplicative factor that minimizes an exponential loss function.

**Exponential loss at iteration $t$:**
$$
L_t = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \exp\!\big(-y_i F_t(x_i)\big)
$$
where $F_t(x) = F_{t-1}(x) + \alpha_t h_t(x)$.

Expand the loss:
$$
L_t = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \exp\!\big(-y_i F_{t-1}(x_i)\big) \cdot \exp\!\big(-\alpha_t y_i h_t(x_i)\big)
$$

Let $w_i^{(t)} \propto \exp(-y_i F_{t-1}(x_i))$. Then:
$$
L_t = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i^{(t)} \exp\!\big(-\alpha_t y_i h_t(x_i)\big)
$$

Splitting the sum into correctly classified ($y_i h_t(x_i) = +1$) and misclassified ($y_i h_t(x_i) = -1$) subsets:
$$
L_t = e^{-\alpha_t} \sum_{y_i = h_t} w_i^{(t)} + e^{\alpha_t} \sum_{y_i \neq h_t} w_i^{(t)}
$$

Let $W_{\text{correct}} = \sum_{y_i = h_t} w_i^{(t)}$ and $W_{\text{wrong}} = \sum_{y_i \neq h_t} w_i^{(t)} = \epsilon_t$ (weighted error). Note that $W_{\text{correct}} = 1 - \epsilon_t$ (after normalization).

Take derivative with respect to $\alpha_t$ and set to zero:
$$
\frac{dL_t}{d\alpha_t} = -e^{-\alpha_t}(1 - \epsilon_t) + e^{\alpha_t} \epsilon_t = 0
$$

Solving:
$$
e^{\alpha_t} \epsilon_t = e^{-\alpha_t}(1 - \epsilon_t)
$$

Multiply both sides by $e^{\alpha_t}$:
$$
e^{2\alpha_t} \epsilon_t = 1 - \epsilon_t
$$

Take natural log of both sides:
$$
2\alpha_t = \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - \epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t}\right)
$$

Therefore:
$$
\alpha_t = \frac{1}{2} \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - \epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t}\right) \quad \blacksquare
$$

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Pipeline (Titanic Dataset)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following exhaustive preprocessing pipeline is the **de facto KTU 2024 lab standard**. Each step must appear in the lab record.

**Step 1 — Load libraries and data:**
Import `pandas`, `numpy`, `seaborn`, `sklearn` modules for preprocessing, model selection, and ensemble classifiers.

**Step 2 — Initial inspection:** Use `df.info()`, `df.describe()`, `df.isnull().sum()` to identify missing values and dtype mismatches.

**Step 3 — Drop non-informative columns:** `Name`, `Ticket`, `Cabin` are removed. `Cabin` has >75% missing values; imputation is not meaningful.

**Step 4 — Handle missing values:**
- `Age`: Impute with **median** (robust to outliers).
- `Embarked`: Impute with **mode** ('S' is most frequent).
- Drop rows with missing `Fare` if any (rare in training set).

**Step 5 — Encode categorical variables:**
- `Sex`: Binary map $\{$male: 0, female: 1$\}$.
- `Embarked`: One-hot encode into `Embarked_C`, `Embarked_Q`, `Embarked_S` (drop one to avoid dummy variable trap).

**Step 6 — Feature engineering (optional, KTU bonus):**
- `FamilySize = SibSp + Parch + 1`
- `IsAlone = 1 if FamilySize == 1 else 0`

**Step 7 — Train-test split:** 80/20 split with `random_state = 42` for reproducibility, `stratify = y` to preserve class balance.

**Step 8 — Standardize numeric features:** Apply `StandardScaler` to `Age`, `Fare` (critical for distance-based models, less so for trees).

**Step 9 — Train ensemble models:**
- Bagging: `BaggingClassifier(base_estimator=DecisionTreeClassifier(), n_estimators=100, random_state=42)`
- Random Forest: `RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, max_depth=5, random_state=42)`
- AdaBoost: `AdaBoostClassifier(n_estimators=100, learning_rate=1.0, random_state=42)`
- Gradient Boosting: `GradientBoostingClassifier(n_estimators=100, learning_rate=0.1, max_depth=3, random_state=42)`
- XGBoost: `XGBClassifier(n_estimators=100, learning_rate=0.1, use_label_encoder=False, eval_metric='logloss')`

**Step 10 — Evaluate:** Use `accuracy_score`, `classification_report` (precision, recall, F1), and `confusion_matrix`.

## 3.4 Comparative Performance Matrix (Typical KTU Lab Results)

| Model | Train Acc | Test Acc | Precision | Recall | F1-Score | Training Time |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Decision Tree (baseline) | 0.978 | 0.788 | 0.78 | 0.74 | 0.76 | Fast |
| **Bagging (DT base)** | 0.972 | 0.821 | 0.81 | 0.78 | 0.79 | Medium |
| **Random Forest** | 0.865 | 0.838 | 0.83 | 0.80 | 0.81 | Medium |
| **AdaBoost** | 0.848 | 0.832 | 0.82 | 0.79 | 0.80 | Medium |
| **Gradient Boosting** | 0.882 | 0.849 | 0.84 | 0.82 | 0.83 | Slow |
| **XGBoost** | 0.901 | 0.866 | 0.85 | 0.84 | 0.84 | Slow |

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Bagging reduces the **variance** of the deep Decision Tree (train acc drops from 0.978 to 0.972, but test acc rises from 0.788 to 0.821 — strong evidence of variance reduction). Boosting models extract the highest test accuracy through sequential bias correction.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Machine Learning Pipeline (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Load Titanic Dataset] --> nodeB[Initial EDA: df.info, df.describe]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Missing Values?}
    nodeC -->|Yes| nodeD[Impute: Age median, Embarked mode]
    nodeC -->|No| nodeE[Drop Non-Informative Columns]
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF[Encode Categoricals: Sex map, Embarked one-hot]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Feature Engineering: FamilySize, IsAlone]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Train Test Split 80 20 with stratify]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Standardize Numeric Features]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Bagging Classifier]
    nodeI --> nodeK[AdaBoost Classifier]
    nodeI --> nodeL[Gradient Boosting Classifier]
    nodeI --> nodeM[XGBoost Classifier]
    nodeJ --> nodeN[Evaluate: Accuracy, F1, Confusion Matrix]
    nodeK --> nodeN
    nodeL --> nodeN
    nodeM --> nodeN
    nodeN --> nodeO[Compare Models and Plot Results]
    nodeO --> nodeP[End]
```

## 4.2 Bagging vs Boosting — Block-Level Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph subgraph1[Bagging Parallel Architecture]
        direction TB
        bagD[Original Dataset D] --> bagS1[Bootstrap Sample 1]
        bagD --> bagS2[Bootstrap Sample 2]
        bagD --> bagS3[Bootstrap Sample B]
        bagS1 --> bagM1[Base Learner h1]
        bagS2 --> bagM2[Base Learner h2]
        bagS3 --> bagM3[Base Learner hB]
        bagM1 --> bagA[Maj Vote / Average]
        bagM2 --> bagA
        bagM3 --> bagA
        bagA --> bagO[Final Prediction]
    end
    subgraph subgraph2[Boosting Sequential Architecture]
        direction TB
        boostD[Dataset with Weights w0] --> boostM1[Learner h1]
        boostM1 --> boostU[Update Weights: Up weight errors]
        boostU --> boostM2[Learner h2]
        boostM2 --> boostU2[Update Weights Again]
        boostU2 --> boostM3[Learner hT]
        boostM1 --> boostF[Weighted Sum of alpha t h t]
        boostM2 --> boostF
        boostM3 --> boostF
        boostF --> boostO[Final Prediction]
    end
```

## 4.3 Decision Boundary Intuition (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Single Decision Tree] -->|High Variance| nodeB[Overfitted Jagged Boundary]
    nodeC[Bagging of 100 Trees] -->|Reduced Variance| nodeD[Smoother Stable Boundary]
    nodeE[AdaBoost Sequential] -->|Reduced Bias| nodeF[Tight Boundary Hugs True Pattern]
    nodeG[Gradient Boosting] -->|Bias and Variance Reduced| nodeH[Highly Refined Boundary]
    nodeB -.->|KTU Insight| nodeI[Train acc high, Test acc low]
    nodeD -.->|KTU Insight| nodeJ[Train and Test acc balanced]
    nodeF -.->|KTU Insight| nodeK[Best test accuracy typically]
    nodeH -.->|KTU Insight| nodeL[Best F1 score typically]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Bagging and Boosting ensemble methods.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Bagging | Boosting |
|---|---|---|
| Training style | Parallel (independent) | Sequential (dependent) |
| Goal | Variance reduction | Bias reduction |
| Sample selection | Bootstrap (random with replacement) | All data, but reweighted |
| Weight handling | Equal weights on models | Weighted by accuracy $\alpha_t$ |
| Base learners | Strong, complex (e.g., deep trees) | Weak learners (e.g., stumps) |
| Susceptibility to overfitting | Lower | Higher (needs shrinkage) |
| Example | Random Forest | AdaBoost, XGBoost |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often confuse the *goal* of the two methods. Memorize: **Bagging ⇒ Variance**, **Boosting ⇒ Bias**.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2023]`
**Explain the role of the learning rate ($\nu$) in Gradient Boosting. What happens if it is set too high or too low?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The learning rate $\nu$ (shrinkage factor) scales the contribution of each newly added base learner to the ensemble:

$$
F_t(x) = F_{t-1}(x) + \nu \cdot h_t(x)
$$

- **Too high** ($\nu \to 1$): The model overshoots, fails to converge, and **overfits** the training data. Test accuracy drops sharply.
- **Too low** ($\nu \to 0$): The model learns very slowly, requires a **large $T$** (more estimators) to fit, increasing training time. Risk of **underfitting** if $T$ is also kept small.
- **Optimal** ($\nu \in [0.01, 0.1]$): Bias is reduced gradually; combines well with higher $T$ for regularization (a trade-off formalized in the **bias-variance-noise decomposition**).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **AdaBoost algorithm** in detail, including initialization, weight update, and final prediction. Derive the expression for the learner weight $\alpha_t$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Implement Bagging and AdaBoost classifiers on the Titanic dataset. Tabulate the **accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score** for both models. Discuss which performs better and justify why. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution

**Part (a) — AdaBoost Algorithm (7 marks):**

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Initialize** instance weights: $w_i^{(0)} = 1/N$ for $i = 1, \ldots, N$.
2. **For $t = 1$ to $T$:**
   - Train weak learner $h_t(x)$ on the weighted training data.
   - Compute weighted error: $\epsilon_t = \sum_i w_i^{(t)} \mathbb{1}\{h_t(x_i) \neq y_i\}$.
   - If $\epsilon_t \geq 0.5$, stop (learner is worse than random).
   - Compute learner weight: $\alpha_t = \frac{1}{2} \ln\!\big(\frac{1-\epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t}\big)$.
   - Update instance weights: $w_i^{(t+1)} = w_i^{(t)} \exp(-\alpha_t y_i h_t(x_i))$.
   - Normalize: $w_i^{(t+1)} \leftarrow w_i^{(t+1)} / \sum_j w_j^{(t+1)}$.
3. **Final prediction:** $H(x) = \text{sign}\big(\sum_{t=1}^{T} \alpha_t h_t(x)\big)$.

**Derivation of $\alpha_t$ (already shown in Section 3.2):**
Starting from the exponential loss $L_t = \sum_i w_i^{(t)} \exp(-\alpha_t y_i h_t(x_i))$, splitting into correct/incorrect subsets, differentiating with respect to $\alpha_t$, and setting $\frac{dL_t}{d\alpha_t} = 0$ yields:
$$
\alpha_t = \frac{1}{2} \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - \epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t}\right)
$$

**Part (b) — Implementation and Comparison (7 marks):**

Implementation steps (Python code is appropriate for this CS-lab course):

```python
from sklearn.ensemble import BaggingClassifier, AdaBoostClassifier
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, classification_report

# After preprocessing (Section 3.3 pipeline)
bag = BaggingClassifier(estimator=DecisionTreeClassifier(),
                        n_estimators=100, random_state=42)
ada = AdaBoostClassifier(n_estimators=100, learning_rate=1.0, random_state=42)

bag.fit(X_train, y_train)
ada.fit(X_train, y_train)

y_pred_bag = bag.predict(X_test)
y_pred_ada = ada.predict(X_test)

print("Bagging:", accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred_bag))
print("AdaBoost:", accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred_ada))
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred_bag))
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred_ada))
```

**Tabulated Result:**

| Model | Accuracy | Precision | Recall | F1-Score |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bagging (DT base) | 0.821 | 0.81 | 0.78 | 0.79 |
| AdaBoost | 0.832 | 0.82 | 0.79 | 0.80 |

**Discussion:** AdaBoost typically outperforms Bagging on Titanic because:
1. The dataset has **strong signal features** (`Sex`, `Pclass`, `Fare`) that AdaBoost can iteratively refine.
2. AdaBoost reduces **bias**, which dominates error here since each individual tree underfits as a stump.
3. Bagging reduces variance — but the base Decision Tree is *not* severely overfitting after `max_depth=5` constraint, so variance reduction yields smaller gains.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]`

**(a)** Explain **Gradient Boosting** with a focus on how it fits each new learner to the negative gradient of the loss function. Show the squared-error case explicitly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare Random Forest (bagging variant) and XGBoost (boosting variant) on the Titanic dataset. Present a **bar chart** of accuracies and discuss why XGBoost generally wins. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution

**Part (a) — Gradient Boosting Derivation (7 marks):**

**Forward stagewise additive modeling:** We seek to minimize
$$
L = \sum_{i=1}^{N} L\big(y_i, F(x_i)\big)
$$
by iteratively adding base learners.

At iteration $t$, the current ensemble is $F_{t-1}(x)$. We add $h_t(x)$ scaled by $\nu$:
$$
F_t(x) = F_{t-1}(x) + \nu \cdot h_t(x)
$$

The **optimal** $h_t(x)$ is the one that minimizes the residual loss. Expanding $L(y_i, F_{t-1}(x_i) + \nu h_t(x_i))$ via Taylor series to first order:
$$
L \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ L(y_i, F_{t-1}(x_i)) + \nu \cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial F}\bigg|_{F_{t-1}} \cdot h_t(x_i) \right]
$$

Since the first term is constant w.r.t. $h_t$, we minimize:
$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{\partial L}{\partial F}\bigg|_{F_{t-1}} \cdot h_t(x_i)
$$

This is equivalent (negated) to fitting $h_t(x)$ to the **negative gradient** (pseudo-residuals):
$$
r_i^{(t)} = -\frac{\partial L(y_i, F(x_i))}{\partial F(x_i)} \bigg|_{F = F_{t-1}}
$$

**Squared Error Case:** $L(y, F) = \frac{1}{2}(y - F)^2$, so:
$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial F} = F - y \quad \Rightarrow \quad r_i^{(t)} = y_i - F_{t-1}(x_i)
$$

Thus Gradient Boosting on squared error fits each new tree to the **actual residuals** — recovering the classical **least-squares boosting** of Friedman (2001).

**Part (b) — Random Forest vs XGBoost Comparison (7 marks):**

```python
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from xgboost import XGBClassifier
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rf = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=200, max_depth=5, random_state=42)
xgb = XGBClassifier(n_estimators=200, learning_rate=0.05,
                    max_depth=3, use_label_encoder=False,
                    eval_metric='logloss', random_state=42)

rf.fit(X_train, y_train)
xgb.fit(X_train, y_train)

acc_rf  = accuracy_score(y_test, rf.predict(X_test))
acc_xgb = accuracy_score(y_test, xgb.predict(X_test))

plt.bar(['Random Forest', 'XGBoost'], [acc_rf, acc_xgb])
plt.ylabel('Accuracy')
plt.title('Titanic Survival Prediction')
plt.savefig('comparison.png')
```

**Expected Output Bar Chart:**

```
Random Forest  | ████████████████ 0.838
XGBoost        | █████████████████ 0.866
```

**Why XGBoost Wins:**

1. **Sequential bias correction:** XGBoost refines errors of previous trees one at a time, while RF just averages independent trees.
2. **Second-order gradients:** XGBoost uses both gradient and Hessian (second derivative) for more accurate step sizes.
3. **Built-in regularization:** L1/L2 penalties on leaf weights prevent overfitting more aggressively than RF's bootstrap variance reduction alone.
4. **Shrinkage + subsampling:** Both $\nu$ and column subsampling further regularize XGBoost.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often lose marks by (1) forgetting to standardize numeric features, (2) using `random_state` inconsistently across models, (3) not stratifying the split leading to class imbalance, (4) reporting only accuracy — KTU 2024 scheme **requires** precision, recall, F1, and confusion matrix. Always produce a `classification_report` in the lab record.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam Day:**

- **Bagging** = **Bootstrap AGGregatING** → parallel, variance reduction, majority vote.
- **Boosting** = sequential, bias reduction, weighted sum.
- **Random Forest** = Bagging + feature subsampling (column sampling at each split).
- **AdaBoost** uses **exponential loss**; $\alpha_t = \frac{1}{2}\ln\frac{1-\epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t}$.
- **Gradient Boosting** fits each new tree to the **negative gradient** (residuals for squared error).
- **XGBoost** = Gradient Boosting + 2nd-order Taylor expansion + L1/L2 regularization + shrinkage.
- **Learning rate $\nu$**: small $\nu$ → slow learning, needs more trees; large $\nu$ → fast but overfits.
- **Titanic benchmark**: target accuracy $\geq$ **0.83** indicates a competent model.
- Always **stratify** the train-test split (`stratify=y`).
- **OOB (Out-of-Bag) score** in Bagging gives a free validation estimate (~36% of samples are OOB per tree).
- **Feature importance** via `.feature_importances_` should be plotted in the lab record.
- **Confusion matrix** is mandatory in KTU lab evaluations.
- **Hyperparameters to tune** in lab: `n_estimators`, `max_depth`, `learning_rate`, `min_samples_split`, `subsample`.
- **Never** drop the `Survived` column accidentally — it is the target.
- **Hyperparameter grid search** with `GridSearchCV` is bonus marks in KTU 2024 lab evaluations.
- **Class imbalance** check: `Survived` mean in Titanic ≈ 0.384 (38.4% positive class) — mild imbalance, no special handling needed.

---
